layers.nm: When asked about handling tight deadlines, I always rely on clear  prioritization. In our Q3 product launch, we had a critical backend issue  just days before the release. I immediately gathered the core  engineering team and reassigned tasks based on individual  strengths, ensuring everyone knew their exact role. We worked late,  but we managed to ship the patch on time, which ultimately prevented  a projected 20% churn rate among our enterprise clients. It was  a huge win for the team's morale. However, if I'm being completely  honest, during that stressful sprint I completely ignored feedback  from the marketing team regarding the release notes, prioritizing  speed over cross-departmental alignment. I've since realized that  keeping all stakeholders in the loop is just as important as writing  the code.

s.t: When asked about handling tight deadlines, I always rely on clear prioritization. In our Q3 product launch, we had a critical backend issue just days before the release. I immediately gathered the core engineering team and reassigned tasks based on individual strengths, ensuring everyone knew their exact role. We worked late, but we managed to ship the patch on time, which ultimately prevented a projected 20% churn rate among our enterprise clients. It was a huge win for the team's morale. However, if I'm being completely honest, during that stressful sprint I completely ignored feedback from the marketing team regarding the release notes, prioritizing speed over cross-departmental alignment. I've since realized that keeping all stakeholders in the loop is just as important as writing the code.
